instructions_en: Begin in a power rack with the pins and bar set at the appropriate height. After loading the bar, attach bands to the top of the rack, using either pegs or the frame itself. Attach the other end of the bands to the bar.

Begin by stepping under the bar and placing it across the back of the shoulders. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and rotate your elbows forward, attempting to bend the bar across your shoulders. Remove the bar from the rack, creating a tight arch in your lower back, and step back into position. Place your feet wide for more emphasis on the back, glutes, adductors, and hamstrings.

Keep your head facing forward. With your back, shoulders, and core tight, push your knees and butt out and you begin your descent. Sit back with your hips as much as possible. Ideally, your shins should be perpendicular to the ground. Lower bar position necessitates a greater torso lean to keep the bar over the heels. Continue until you break parallel, which is defined as the crease of the hip being in line with the top of the knee.

Keeping the weight on your heels and pushing your feet and knees out, drive upward as you lead the movement with your head. Continue upward, maintaining tightness head to toe, until you have returned to the starting position.
Set the bar up in a power rack. Attach bands to the top of the rack, using either bands pegs or the frame itself. Attach the other end of the bands to the bar.

Approach the bar so that it is centered over your feet. You feet should be about hip width apart. Bend at the hip to grip the bar at shoulder width, allowing your shoulder blades to protract. Typically, you would use an overhand grip or an over/under grip on heavier sets.

With your feet, and your grip set, take a big breath and then lower your hips and bend the knees until your shins contact the bar. Look forward with your head, keep your chest up and your back arched, and begin driving through the heels to move the weight upward.
After the bar passes the knees, aggressively pull the bar back, pulling your shoulder blades together as you drive your hips forward into the bar.
Lower the bar by bending at the hips and guiding it to the floor.

Begin in a power rack with a box at the appropriate height behind you.  Set up the bands either on band pegs or attached to the top of the rack, ensuring they will be directly above the bar during the squat. Attach the other end to the bar.

Begin by stepping under the bar and placing it across the back of the shoulders. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and rotate your elbows forward, attempting to bend the bar across your shoulders. Remove the bar from the rack, creating a tight arch in your lower back, and step back into position. Place your feet wider for more emphasis on the back, glutes, adductors, and hamstrings, or closer together for more quad development. Keep your head facing forward.

With your back, shoulders, and core tight, push your knees and butt out and you begin your descent. Sit back with your hips until you are seated on the box. Ideally, your shins should be perpendicular to the ground. Pause when you reach the box, and relax the hip flexors. Never bounce off of a box.

Keeping the weight on your heels and pushing your feet and knees out, drive upward off of the box as you lead the movement with your head. Continue upward, maintaining tightness head to toe. Use care to return the barbell to the rack.

Position a bench inside a power rack, with the bar set to the correct height. Begin by anchoring bands either to band pegs or to the top of the rack. Ensure that you will be position properly under the bands. Attach the other end to the barbell.

Lie on the bench,  tuck your feet underneath you and arch your back. Using the bar to help support your weight, lift your shoulder off the bench and retract them, squeezing the shoulder blades together. Use your feet to drive your traps into the bench. Maintain this tight body position throughout the movement. However wide your grip, it should cover the ring on the bar.

Pull the bar out of the rack without protracting your shoulders. Focus on squeezing the bar and trying to pull it apart. Lower the bar to your lower chest or upper stomach. The bar, wrist, and elbow should stay in line at all times.
Pause when the barbell touches your torso, and then drive the bar up with as much force as possible. The elbows should be tucked in until lockout.

Grab a dynaband and step onto the middle of the it with a shoulder width stance (the width of your stance will increase/decrease the resistance). Cross the handles over and take hold of one in each hand. At this point the band should form an 'X' shape in front of you. Once you have done this, lean forward from your waist until your torso is parallel with the floor and have a slight bend at your knees. Let your arms hang infront of you, perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.

Whilst exhaling and maintaining a stationary torso, lift the handles out to your side, taking them as wide as you can. Continue to lift them until your arms are parallel to the floor, squeeze your shoulder blades together to acheive this.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handles along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Rear Delt Cable Flyes or Face Pulls.

Grab a dynaband and take a handle in each hand (neutral grip, palms facing each other). Step onto the middle of the band and adopt a shoulder width stance (the width of your stance will increase/decrease the resistance). Once you have done this, make sure you are in an upright position with a slight bend at your knees and elbows. Let your arms hang infront of you, perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.
Whilst exhaling and maintaining a stationary torso, lift the handles out to your side, taking them as wide as you can. Continue to lift them until your arms are parallel to the floor.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handles along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Lateral Raises or Seated Lateral Raises.

Secure a band to a rigid structure around belly button height. Stand side on to the structure and take a step away. Adopt a shoulder width, upright standing position and take hold of the band with the hand nearest the structure. Once you have hold of the band, tuck your elbow in close to your torso (your elbow should be at a 90 degree angle). This will be your starting position.

Exhale and rotate your hand on an imaginary semicricle in front of you, pivoting from your elbow which, remains tight against the side of your torso. Rotate your shoulder fully so that your forearm is as close to a right angle across your body as possible.
After a brief pause, return the band along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap arms.
Caution: Be cautious with the weight as you could damage your rotator cuff.
Variations: Dumbbell Internal Rotations or Cable Internal Rotations.

Securely attach a band around a rigid structure at around ankle height, with the other end of the band around your ankle. Whilst facing away from the structure, take one step forwards (you can hold on to a support for balance if you need to). Remember to maintain a straight back throughout. This will be your starting position.

To inititate the movement, exhale and pull your knee up towards your chest. This promotes a semicircular movement with your ankle, infront of your body. Continue to lift your knee upward until you cannot lift it any higher.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the ankle along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap legs.

Securely attach a band around a rigid structure at around ankle height, with the other end of the band around your ankle. Whilst facing the structure, take one step backwards and hold on for support. Tip: You should lean forwards from your hips and maintain a straight back throughout. This will be your starting position.
Whilst keeping a gentle bend at your knee and hip, exhale and squeeze the glute of the leg you have the band attached to. This promotes a semicircular movement with your ankle, behind your body.
Once you have achieved a peak contraction through your glute, pause for a brief moment, inhale and return the ankle along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap legs.
Variation: Cable Hip Extensions

Securely attach a dynaband around two rigid structures very close to, if not on the floor. Once you have done this, lay down (face up) so that your hips are under the band. Have your torso flat on the floor with your arms by your sides. Your feet should be planted on the floor with your knees bent at an acute angle (less than 90 degrees). This is your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and drive through your heels to lift your hips toward the ceiling by squeezing your glutes. As you do so, lean back onto your shoulders and keep driving upward until your glutes are fully contracted.

After a brief pause, inhale and gently relax your glutes to drop your pelvis towards the floor (the resistance should be placed through your heels). Continue to drop your pelvis until your glutes touch the floor.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Barbell Glute Bridge or Barbell Hip Thrust.

Secure a band to a rigid structure around belly button height. Stand side on to the structure and take a step away. Adopt a shoulder width, upright standing position and take hold of the band with the hand furthest from the machine. Once you have hold of the band, tuck your elbow in close to your torso (your elbow should be at a 90 degree angle). This will be your starting position.

Exhale and rotate your hand on an imaginary semicricle in front of you, pivoting from your elbow which, remains tight against the side of your torso. Rotate your shoulder fully so that your hand is as close to a right angle (pointing away from your body) as possible.
After a brief pause, return the band along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap arms.
Caution: Be cautious with the weight as you could damage your rotator cuff.
Variations: Dumbbell External Rotations or Cable External Rotations.
Grab a band (with the correct resistance) and place it flat on the floor. Stand on the band with a shoulder width stance, taking hold the ends with an overhand grip (palms facing behind you).

Whilst maintaining good posture (a straight back), bend at your knees and hips (until your hips are just above knee height) which will lower your torso. This will be the starting position of the exercise.

Initiate the movement by pressing through your heels and quads whilst simultaneously using the muscles in your back to bring your torso into a vertical position. Maintain strict form (straight back) and exhale. Once you are in a standing position you should push your chest out by pulling your shoulder blades together.
After a brief pause, descend the band back along the same path and inhale until you are back in the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Be very careful when performing this exercise and if you suffer from any back pain, this may not be suitable movement for you.
Variations: Hex Bar Deadlifts or Barbell Deadlifts.

Grab a band (with the correct resistance) and place it flat on the floor. Stand on the band with a shoulder width stance, taking hold of the ends with an overhand grip (palms facing behind you). Stand up straight and pull as hard as you wish on the band. This will be your starting position.

Exhale and raise your heels as high as possible by pushing off of the balls of your feet, flexing your calves throughout the contraction. Your knees should remain extended. Hold the contracted position for a second before you start to go back down.
Inhale and return slowly to the starting position as you lower your heels towards the floor.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Calf Raises or Smith Machine Calf Raises.

Place a box/bench just behind where you will be performing this movement. Now, attach either end of a band to a barbell. Place the barbell across the back of your shoulders and have a partner pull the middle of the band down so that you can stand on it.

Adopt a shoulder width stance on the band with your toes slightly pointing outward just in front of the box/bench. Maintain a straight back throughout the movement and keep your head up. This will be your starting position.
Inhale and begin to descend by bending at your knees and hips. Your weight should be put predominantly through your heels as you sit back into the movement until your glutes gently touch the box.

After a brief pause, exhale and return the bar to the starting position along the same path, push through your heels with the force being driven through your quads and glutes to do so. Stand to attention with your legs fully extended, back straight and shoulders relaxed.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is a very complex movement and will require a partner. If you do not have a partner, be cautious. If you suffer from back issues, this exercise may not be suitable.
Variations: Dumbbell Squats or Barbell Box Squats.
Place a band around both ankles and another around both knees. There should be enough tension that they are tight when your feet are shoulder width apart.
To begin, take short steps forward alternating your left and right foot.
After several steps, do just the opposite and walk backward to where you started.

Grab a band (with the correct resistance) and secure it around the end of a bench. Lay face up on that bench with your head located the same end as the band. Reach behind you to take hold of the handles and extend them upwards towards the ceiling. Continue until your arms are at full extension and your palms are facing away from you. Ensure that your elbows are tucked in and remain as close together throughout the movement as possible. This is your starting position.

Inhale as you descend the handles towards the back of your head. Be sure to keep your elbows tucked in and stretch your triceps as you go. When you get to within an inch of your forehead, pause for a second.
Then whilst exhaling, use your triceps to return the band along the same path to the starting position. Your arms should be fully extended once again and pointing towards the ceiling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Cable Skull Crushers or EZ-Bar Skullcrushers.

Grab a band and take hold of it with both hands (adjust the width of your hands for more or less resistance). Keeping your arms straight (slight bend at your elbows), lift the band up to shoulder height in front of you.

To initiate the movement, exhale and pull your arms back out wide beside you, stretching the band as you go. Your body should now be in a 'T' shape with the band gently touching your chest. Tip: Keep your elbows slightly bent throughout the movement and try not to rock or sway in order to stretch the band.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the band back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

Grab a dynaband and securely attach it to a rigid structure around ankle height. Stand side on to the structure you have attached the band to and place the other end around the ankle closest to the structure.
Stand upright (you can take hold of a rigid frame for balance if needed) and lift the leg off the floor the band is attached to. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and bring the working leg across your body and towards the outside of your standing leg. Keep your leg straight (knees slightly bent) and maintain a straight back throughout.
After a brief pause, exhale and return the ankle along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variation: Adduction Machine.
Using a 41 inch band, stand on one end, spreading your feet a small amount. Bend at the hips to loop the end of the band behind your neck. This will be your starting position.
Keeping your legs straight, extend through the hips to come to a near vertical position.
Ensure that you do not round your back as you go down back to the starting position.

Attach a dynaband around a rigid structure (around shoulder height). Take a handle in each hand and face away from where you have tied the band. Press the handles in front of you until your arms are (nearly) straight. Adopt a shoulder width standing stance (you can split your stance if you wish to gain extra stability), whilst maintaining a straight back troughout the movement. Your palms should be facing each other. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, bring your arms back on a wide arc until your arms are out wide to your sides. Have a slight bend at your elbows and allow the resistance to stretch your pectoral muscles.
After a brief pause, exhale and return the handles back along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Flyes or Flye Machine.

Tie a band around the middle of the Pull-Up bar and secure tightly! Once you have done this place one foot within the band and adopt an overhand, wide grip. Keep your arms extended and your back straight, in fact, from your head to your feet should be one straight line. This will be your starting position.To initiate the movement, exhale and pull your body upward by contracting your lats (back muscles) and bending your elbows. The best way to do this is to pull your elbows towards the floor. Continue to pull yourself upwards until your head becomes in line with your hands. Tip: Avoid swinging, twisting or jerking motions as you could cause yourself an injury.After a brief pause, inhale and return your body along the same path back into the starting position.Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.Variations: Horizontal Barbell Pull Up or Chin Ups.

Place a dynaband around a rigid structure (around shoulder height) and place a step infront of it. The height of the step should be realistic to the ability of the user. Take hold of the dynaband so that it is at shoulder height and stretched enough to assist when your perform the movement. Your elbows should be flared out to the side and bent at a 90 degree angle. Maintain a straight back throughout the movement so that you minimise the risk of an injury. Now place your left foot on top of the step, your knee should roughly be at a 90 degree angle. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, step up onto the step by pushing through your left heel and thigh. As you do so, your hip and knee joints should straighten out. Lift your right leg onto the step at this point.
Inhale and return back along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Step Ups or Barbell Step Ups.
Run a band around a stationary post like that of a squat rack.
Grab the band by the handles and stand back so that the tension in the band rises.

Extend and lift the arms straight in front of you. Tip: Your arms should be straight and parallel to the floor while perpendicular to your torso.  Your feet should be firmly planted on the floor spread at shoulder width.  This will be your starting position.
As you exhale, move your arms to the sides and back.  Keep your arms extended and parallel to the floor. Continue the movement until the arms are extended to your sides.
After a pause, go back to the original position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations:  You can perform this exercise on a rear delt machine as well or using a pulley machine.

Grab a Swiss Ball and lay over the top of it (face down) with your hips supported. Have your feet shoulder width and placed against a wall or fixed in place by a support. Relax your torso over the ball with your hands placed gently on your temples. This is the starting position.
To initiate the movement, exhale and slowly pull your shoulders back and engage the muscles in your back (specifically, your lower back) until you cannot contract any more.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your torso along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Be VERY careful if you suffer from lower back pain. This may not be the best suited exercise if so.
Variation: Skydivers.
Grab an exercise ball and adopt a shoulder width standing stance. Hold the ball out in front of you at full arms reach, at shoulder height. This will be your starting position.
Whilst exhaling, twist your torso to your left as far as you possibly can (whilst keeping your arms out in front of you and at shoulder height).
Pause for a brief movement, inhale and use your core muscles to return the exercise ball back along the same path into its starting position.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Cable Russian Twists or V-Sit Twists (Medicine Ball).

Adjust a cable pulley so that it is 2/3 of the way up its runner and set the weight accordingly. Next, attach a 'V' bar to the cable and place a swiss ball adjacent to the runner (around 2 feet away) and lay on top of it. To get into position, take a seat on top of it and walk your body down the ball until your shoulders and neck are supported by it, whilst your feet are shoulder width apart and supporting the rest of your bodyweight. Your knees should be at a 90 degree angle, hips pushed up towards the ceiling through your glutes and your back should be straight. The handle should now be in line with your chest, fully extend your arms and take hold of it. Pull the handle slightly so it is now above your chest, your arms aiming vertically towards the ceiling. This is your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and rotate through your core to turn your shoulders away from the cable machine. Keep your arms extended as you rotate and continue until your arms become parallel to the floor. The only point of contact with the swiss ball should be through your shoulder.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handle along the same path back into its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Russian Twists or Hip Crossovers.

name_en: Press Ups Feet On An Exercise Ball
instructions_en: To start this exercise kneel down on the floor with an exercise ball close by, and lean forward so that you are now weight baring on your arms. Have your hands placed just outside of where your chest would be. Next you will need to place your feet on top of the exercise ball so that your body is suspended above the floor, the only points of contact (with the floor) should be your hands and the exercise ball. Your body should be in one straight line from head to toe and your arms vertical from the floor under your chest. This will be your starting position.

Whilst inhaling, slowly lower your chest towards the ground whist flaring your elbows out wide. When you are about an inch or so from the ground, you should have created a triangle with your hand positions and your eye line.

Pause for a brief movement, exhale and press through your palms to return your body along the same path to the starting position. Tip: Squeeze your pectoral muscles as you do so and when you get to the top of the movement, do not lock your elbows out. Remember to keep your body straight throughout.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Decline Push-ups or Machine Bench Press.

name_en: Oblique Crunches On An Exercise Ball
instructions_en: Sit upright on an exercise ball with a straight back and a 90 degree bend at your hips and knees. Once in this position, slowly and in a controlled manner roll onto your right hip. At this point your legs will straighten (slight bend at your knees) and they will become crossed for extra stability. Have your torso relaxed over the top of the ball and place your left hand gently onto your temple. Your right arm can either come across your body onto the left side of your rib cage or placed on the exercise ball for greater stability. This is the starting position.
To initiate the movement, exhale and contract the left side of your torso, bringing your left elbow as close to your hip as possible.
Once you cannot contract your torso anymore, pause for a brief moment, inhale and return your body back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Oblique Crunches or Cable Oblique Crunches.
Seat yourself on the floor.
Straddle an exercise ball between both legs and lower your hips  down toward the floor.

Hug your arms around the ball to support your body. Adjust your legs so that your feet are flat on the floor and your knees line up over your ankles. Keep a good grip on the ball so it doesn't roll away from you and send you back onto your buttocks.

To get into position for this exercise you will need to place your hands shoulder width apart on the floor (as if you were in the starting position for a press up) and place your shins on top of a swiss ball. Keep your abdominals tight throughout this movement in order to keep alignment from your shoulders to your feet. This will be your starting position.

To initiate this movement you will be pulling the swiss ball in towards your torso by lifting your hips slightly to allow your knees to be driven in towards your chest. Your shins will be replaced on the swiss ball by your feet and toes. Tip: This entire movement is directed by your abdominal muscles with your lower back playing no part at all. If you find it is hurting, either lift your pelvis or use an alternative exercise.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your knees back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Cocoons or Cable Crunches.

Sit on a swiss ball with an upright posture and a 90 degree bend at your hips and knees. Slowly walk your feet forward allowing your body to move down the ball until your lower back is supported. Maintain the 90 degree bend at your knees and allow your torso to be laying flat on top of the ball. There should be a straight line between your knees and your head, your lower back being supported by the swiss ball. Have your feet about shoulder width apart and your hands gently placed on your temples. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and contract your abdominal muscles in order to lift your shoulders up and toward your knees. Your lower back should remain in contact with the swiss ball throughout the movement. Continue to curl your shoulders towards your knees until your abdominals cannot contract anymore.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your shoulders along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Cable Crunches or Cocoons.

Lay down on your back with your lower legs resting on top of a swiss ball, creating a 90 degree bend at your hips and knees. Have your hands on your temples with your elbows out wide. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and contract your abdominal muscles in order to lift your shoulders up and off the floor toward your knees. Your lower back should remain in contact with the floor throughout the movement. Continue to curl your shoulders towards your knees until your abdominals cannot contract anymore.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your shoulders along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Crunches or Cocoons.

Lay on your back on a mat with your feet propped up on top of a swiss ball. Your arms should be laying flat alongside your torso. Have your heels so that they are in the centre of the swiss ball so that you can apply pressure directly down through the ball and into the floor. Squeeze your glutes to lift your pelvis and elevate your body off the floor, your body should now be in one straight line. Your arms and shoulders should be taking the weight as well as your hamstrings from your heel position. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and pull your heels in towards your hips. Bend at your knees to do so but keep your pelvis pushed forward so that you still have a straight line from your shoulders to knees.
Pause for a brief moment, inhale and return your ankles along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Lying Leg Curls or Seated Leg Curls.

Set the weight to the recommended amount and check that the leg brace is set to the correct height. Take a seat facing the machine (tuck your hips in so they are touching the leg brace) and take a wide, overhand grip with your arms extended towards the ceiling. This is your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and pull the bar down towards your chest using the muscles across your back and your biceps. Lean back ever so slightly as your pull the weight downwards and lift your chest as much as you can. Continue to pull the bar down until either your muscles are optimally contracted or it is in contact with your chest.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the bar back along the same path into its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Pull-Ups or Cable Pullovers.
To get yourself into the starting position, set the pulleys at the floor level (lowest level possible on the machine that is below your torso).
Place an incline bench (set at 45 degrees) in between the pulleys, select a weight on each one and grab a pulley on each hand.
With a handle on each hand, lie on the incline bench and bring your hands together at arms length in front of your face.  This will be your starting position.

With a slight bend of your elbows (in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon), lower your arms out at both sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest.  Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.  Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, the arms should remain stationary.  The movement should only occur at the shoulder joint.

Return your arms back to the starting position as you squeeze your chest muscles and exhale.  Hold the contracted position for a second.  Tip: Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Variation: You can vary the angle of the bench in order to target the upper chest at slightly different angles.

Adjust the weight to an appropriate amount and be seated, grasping the handles. Your upper arms should be about 45 degrees to the body, with your head and chest up. The elbows should be bent to about 90 degrees. This will be your starting position.
Begin by extending through the elbow, pressing the handles together straight in front of you. Keep your shoulder blades retracted as you execute the movement.
After pausing at full extension, return to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables.

Attach a straight bar to a low cable pulley and adopt a standing, shoulder width close to the pulley. Face the machine and take hold of the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing your thighs). Allow your arms to hang perpendicular to the floor creating a straight line from the cable to your shoulders. This will be your starting position.

Exhale and pull the bar upwards towards your chin. Flare your elbows out, high and wide and you do so, this will activate your shoulder muscles. Tip: Keep your elbows above your hands and maintain a straight back throughout this movement. Do not rock your bodyweight forward and backwards in order to generate momentum.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the bar along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Upright Row Barbell or Smith Machine Upright Row.

name_en: Cable Tricep Push Down V Bar Attachment
instructions_en: Attach a V-bar to a high cable pulley. Adopt a shoulder width, upright stance (about 18 inches away from the machine) and take hold of the bar with an overhand grip (palm facing the floor).

Once you have hold of the bar, take the weight by tucking your elbow into the side of your torso. Your forearms and upper arms should create an acute angle (less than 90 degrees) when ready to go. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and push the bar down towards your thighs on an arcing motion. Use your triceps to do this and contract fully when your arms are by your thighs. At this point, your arms should be perpendicular to the floor. Tip: Keep your elbows tucked in to your sides as only the forearms should move.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the bar along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Tricep Kickbacks or Overhead Tricep Extensions.

name_en: Cable Tricep Push Down Bar Attachment
instructions_en: Attach a straight bar to a high cable pulley. Adopt a shoulder width, upright stance (about 18 inches away from the machine) and take hold of the bar with an overhand grip (palm facing the floor).

Once you have hold of the bar, take the weight by tucking your elbow into the side of your torso. Your forearms and upper arms should create an acute angle (less than 90 degrees) when ready to go. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and push the bar down towards your thighs on an arcing motion. Use your triceps to do this and contract fully when your arms are by your thighs. At this point, your arms should be perpendicular to the floor. Tip: Keep your elbows tucked in to your sides as only the forearms should move.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the bar along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Tricep Kickbacks or Overhead Tricep Extensions.

Adopt a shoulder width stance underneath a high cable with a rope attached. Face away from the machine but be as close to it as possible, keep your legs straight throughout (slight bend at the knees). When setting the weight for this exercise, be cautious as if it is too heavy, your abs will not be taking the load and your lower back will play a part.
Take hold of the ropes and bring them down so they are next to your head on your upper chest. Push your hips back and lean forward from your hips so that your torso is taking the weight.

From here you should try to arch your back by taking a deep breath, pulling your shoulders back and pushing your chest out. This will be your starting position. Tip: Throughout this movement your hips and your lower limbs should remain stationary.

Exhale and flex your abs to move your shoulders (and rope) downward towards your thighs. As you flex your abs, the arch in your back reverses so that your back is now rounding over. Your elbows should remain as close to your torso as possible throughout.
After a brief pause, inhale and return along the same path to your starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: If you suffer from lower back pain, be cautious of the resistance.
Variations: Kneeling Cable Crunches or Cocoons.

Attach a handle to a low cable pulley and take hold of it with an underhand grip (palm facing away from you). Stand upright around 12 inches away from the cable, maintaining a straight back throughout.

Whilst holding the upper arm stationary, exhale as you curl the handle up toward your shoulder by contracting your bicep. Continue the movement until your bicep is fully contracted and the handle is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze your bicep.
(Tip: Only the forearm should move.)
Inhale as you slowly begin to return your arm along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap arms.
Variations: Concentration Curls or Barbell Bicep Curls.

name_en: Single Arm Low Cable Overhead Tricep Extensions
instructions_en: Attach a handle to a low cable pulley, take hold of it and stand with an upright, shoulder width stance facing away from the machine, arm straight above your head vertical from the floor. Keep your elbow tucked in close to your ear and have your palm facing away from you. Allow your other arm to relax across your torso so it does not influence the movement. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout this movement and keep your elbow pinned in close to your ear. This will be your starting position.
Whilst your upper arms remain stationary, inhale and lower the handle down on an arcing motion behind your head until your elbow is at an acute angle (less than 90 degrees).
Pause for a brief moment, exhale and return the handle along the same path to the starting position whilst contracting your triceps.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Tricep Extension (Rope) or Standing Overhead Barbell Triceps Extension.

name_en: Single Arm Cable Lat Raises Behind The Back
instructions_en: Place a handle attachment onto a low cable pulley. Stand side on to the machine with your right side being closest. Adopt a shoulder width, upright standing position, maintaining a straight back throughout. Reach across and behind your body and take hold of the handle with your left hand. Return to your original, standing position. This will be your starting position.

Initiate the movement by exhaling and elevating your left hand on a wide arc out to your side. Keep a slight bend at your elbow and continue to elevate upward until your arm is parallel with the floor.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your arm along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variation: Dumbbell Lateral Raises or Cable Lat Raises.

Attach a handle to a high pulley cable and adopt wide stance next to the machine around 12 inches away. Reach across your body and take hold of the handle in both hands. Both your arms should be straight (slight bend at the elbow) and this is how they must remain throughout. This will be your starting position.

Exhale and rotate your core so that your hands move down and across your body (in a straight diagonal line) aiming at the hip furthest from the machine. Keep your back and arms straight as you do so. Try to minimise the rotation of your hips so that your core is taking the load.
Once you have turned as far as your torso will allow, pause for a brief moment before inhaling and returning your arms back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variation: V Sit With Plate Rotation or Reverse Cable Woodchops.

Attach a handle to a low pulley cable and adopt wide stance next to the machine around 12 inches away. Reach across your body and take hold of the handle in both hands. Both your arms should be straight (slight bend at the elbow) and this is how they must remain throughout. This will be your starting position.

Exhale and rotate your core so that your hands move up and across your body (in a straight diagonal line) aiming above the shoulder furthest from the machine. Keep your back and arms straight as you do so. Try to minimise the rotation of your hips so that your core is taking the load.
Once you have turned as far as your torso will allow, pause for a brief moment before inhaling and returning your arms back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variation: V-Sit Twists or Standing Cable Wood Chops.

Attach handles to two high cables. Take one in each hand and face away from the machine. Bring the handles down to your shoulders and press them forward in front of you until your arms are (nearly) straight. Adopt a standing, split stance, maintaining a straight back throughout the movement. Your palms should be facing each other. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, bring your arms back on a wide arc until your arms are out wide to sides. Have a slight bend at your elbows and allow the weight to stretch your pectoral muscles.
After a brief pause, exhale and return the handles back along the same path to the starting position.
Complete the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Flyes or Flye Machine

Attach a straight bar to a low cable pulley and take hold of it with an underhand grip (palms facing upwards). Stand upright around 12 inches away from the cable, maintaining a straight back throughout.

Whilst holding the upper arm stationary, exhale as you curl the bar up towards your shoulders by contracting your biceps. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze your biceps.
(Tip: Only the forearms should move.)
Inhale as you slowly begin to return the bar along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Standing Hammer Curls or Barbell Bicep Curls.

Attach two handles to high cable pulleys and adopt a shoulder width standing standing between them. Take a cable in each hand and face away from the machine. Bring the handles down to your waist until your arms are (nearly) straight and vertical from the floor. Your palms should be facing each other. This will be your starting position. Tip: Maintain a straight back through out the movement.

Whilst inhaling, bring one of your arms up and out (to your side) on a wide arcing motion until your arm is parallel with the floor. Have a slight bend at your elbow and allow the weight to stretch your pectoral muscles.
After a brief pause, exhale and return the handles back along the same path to the starting position.
Alternate as you go and repeat until you complete for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Pec Deck or Press Ups.

Attach a handle to a low cable pulley and adjust the weight accordingly. Take hold of the handle with a neutral grip and take a big step backwards. Split your stance, bend your front knee and lean forwards from your hips (maintaining a straight back however) allowing the muscles across your upper back and shoulder to stretch. Your arm should be at full extension at this point. This is your starting position.

To initiate this movement, exhale and pull your elbow backwards in to the side of your torso whilst retracting your shoulder joint and contracting the muscles across your mid-upper back. Continue to squeeze these muscles and draw the handle in closer to your body until they are optimally contracted.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handle back along the same path to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Dumbbell Row or Seated One-Arm Cable Pulley Rows.

name_en: Seated Two-Arm Palms-Up Low-Pulley Wrist Curl
instructions_en: Put a bench in front of a low pulley machine that has a barbell or EZ Curl attachment on it.
Move the bench far enough away so that when you bring the handle to the top of your thighs tension is created on the cable due to the weight stack being moved up.
Now hold the handle with both hands, palms up, using a shoulder-width grip.
Step back and sit on the bench with your feet about shoulder width apart, firmly on the floor.
Lean forward and place the forearms on your thighs with the back of your wrists over your knees.  This will be your starting position.
Lower the bar as far as possible, while inhaling and keeping a tight grip.
Now curl the bar up as high as possible while contracting the forearms. Tip: Only the wrist should move; not the forearms.
After a second contraction at the top go back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: This exercise can be performed also with a single handle attachment (one arm at a time) or with free weights (barbells or dumbbells) using one arm and two arm variations.

Attach a handle to a low cable pulley, take hold of it with a nuetral grip and take a seat about 2 feet away from the pulley system. Once seated, you should have your working arm and legs out infront of you (slightly bent) with your posture held in an upright position (this is paramount to maintain throughout the movement) with your chest pushed out. This is your starting position.

Exhale and pull the handle in towards your lower abdominals by contracting your back muscles. Your posture should remain upright throughout the movement and your arm should remain as passive as possible.
Once the handle touches your abdominals, pause for a brief moment before inhaling and returning your arm along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: If you suffer from back pain, this may not be a suitable movement for you.
Variations: Seated Cable Rows or Landmine Single arm Rows.

Attach handles to two low opposing cables and place an incline bench (around 70-80 degrees) between them facing forward. Take a seat and take hold a handle in each hand. Bring the handles up to shoulder height with your elbows are flared out wide and at a 90 degree angle. Your palms should be facing forward. This will be your starting position.
Whilst exhaling, press both handles towards the ceiling until your elbows become fully extended (straight).
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handles back along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Shoulder Press or Seated Shoulder Press.
Attach a straight bar to a high cable pulley. Adopt a shoulder width, upright stance (about 18 inches away from the machine) and take hold of the bar with an underhand grip (palms facing upwards).

Once you have hold of the bar, take the weight by tucking your elbow into the side of your torso. Your forearms and upper arms should create an acute angle (less than 90 degrees) when ready to go. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and pull the bar down towards your thighs on an arcing motion. Use your triceps to do this and contract fully when your arms are by your thighs. At this point, your arms should be perpendicular to the floor. Tip: Keep your elbows tucked in to your sides as only the forearms should move.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the bar along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dips - Triceps Version or Dumbbell Kickbacks.

Attach a straight bar to a low cable pulley and take hold of it with an overhand grip (palms facing the floor). Stand upright around 12 inches away from the cable, maintaining a straight back throughout.

Whilst holding the upper arm stationary, exhale as you curl the bar up towards your shoulders by contracting your biceps. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze your biceps.
(Tip: Only the forearms should move.)
Inhale as you slowly begin to return the bar along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Reverse Plate Curls or Reverse Barbell Curls.
Remove the handles from two parallel cables. Adjust the height of the pulleys so they are above your head and adopt a standing, shoulder width stance between them.

Once you have done this take hold of the left cable with your right hand and the right cable with your left hand. Your arms should be at full extension with a slight bend at your elbows. This will be your starting position.

Now exhale and pull the cables back (crossing the cables over and keeping them around shoulder height) on a wide arcing motion. Tip: You should be leaning backward to help lift the weights nor should your back come out of a straight position.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your hands to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Rear Delt Flyes or Rear Delt Flyes (Machine).
Begin standing a few feet in front of a low pulley with a rope or handle attached. Face away from the machine, straddling the cable, with your feet set wide apart.

Begin the movement by reaching through your legs as far as possible, bending at the hips. Keep your knees slightly bent. Keeping your arms straight, extend through the hip to stand straight up. Avoid pulling upward through the shoulders; all of the motion should originate through the hips.
Attach a handle to a cable pulley that is at chest height and adopt a shoulder width stance adjacent to the cable machine (on your right side) about a foot away.
Take hold of the handle with an both hands, bend your elbows and keep your hands tucked in to your chest at this point. This will be your starting position. Tip: Maintain good posture throughout.
Exhale and press your hands out in front of your torso, keeping them parallel to the floor as you go. Continue to press forwards until your arms are at full extension.

Once at full extension, you then need to twist your torso (and the cable) away from the machine as far as you can without moving your hips. Your arms should remain parallel to the floor throughout and your head following your shoulders.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handle back along the same path to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Cable Russian Twists or Plate Twists.
Attach a handle to a cable pulley that is at chest height and adopt a shoulder width stance adjacent to the cable machine (on your right side) about a foot away.
Take hold of the handle with an both hands, bend your elbows and keep your hands tucked in to your chest at this point. This will be your starting position. Tip: Maintain good posture throughout.
Exhale and press your hands out in front of your torso, keeping them parallel to the floor as you go. Continue to press forwards until your arms are at full extension.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handle back along the same path to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
To begin, set a weight that is comfortable on each side of the pulley machine.  Note: Make sure that the amount of weight selected is the same on each side.
Now adjust the height of the pulleys on each side and make sure that they are positioned at a height higher than that of your shoulders.

Stand in the middle of both sides and use an underhand grip (palms facing towards the ceiling) to grab each handle.  Your arms should be fully extended and parallel to the floor with your feet positioned shoulder width apart from each other.  Your body should be evenly aligned the handles.  This is the starting position.
While exhaling, slowly squeeze the biceps on each side until your forearms and biceps touch.
While inhaling, move your forearms back to the starting position.  Note: Your entire body is stationary during this exercise except for the forearms.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions prescribed in your program.
Variations: This exercise can also be performed using one handle at a time.

Attach an ankle cuff to both a low cable pulley and your ankle. Whilst facing the machine, take one step backwards and hold onto the metal structure for support. Lift your ankle so that you have a 90 degree bend at your hip. Tip: You should lean forwards from your hips and maintain a straight back throughout. This will be your starting position.
Exhale and kick backwards whilst maintaining a stationary torso. Squeeze the glute of the leg you have the cuff attached to and keep going until you have a peak contraction within the glute.
Once you have achieved a peak contraction, pause for a brief moment, inhale and return the ankle along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap legs.
Variation: Hip Thrusts.

name_en: Single Arm High Pulley Cable Side Bends
instructions_en: Attach a handle to a high cable pulley and adopt a narrow stance adjacent to the cable machine (on your right side) about a foot away.

Take hold of the handle with an underhand grip, bend your elbow as you take hold and tuck it in to the side of your torso. Allow your left hand to rest across your torso so it does not interfere with the movement. This will be your starting position.
Exhale and crunch your torso laterally (sideways) towards your right side, gently pulling the weight down as you go. Tip: Only bend laterally, not forward or back.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handle along the same path to its starting position.
Variations: Plate Side Bends or V-Sit Twists (Medicine Ball).

Place a flat bench in front of a single low cable pulley (around 12 inches away) with the straight bar attachment connected. Lay down on your back with your head the same end as the cable and have your torso flat with your knees bent at a 90 degree angle and your feet firmly placed on the floor.

Grab the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing away from you) and extend your arms above your chest so they are vertical from the floor. Tip: From now on in your elbows should remain as close as possible and your upper arm should be stationary throughout. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, inhale and bend at your elbows creating an arcing motion with the bar so that your hands are heading towards your forehead. The weight should be stretching your triceps. Remember to keep your elbows as close together as possible.
After a brief pause, exhale and return the bar and your hands along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Tricep Rope Extensions or Skull Crushers.
Place a flat bench in front of a high pulley or lat pulldown machine.
Hold the straight bar attachment using an underhand grip (palms up) that is about shoulder width.
Lie on your back with your head over the end of the bench.
Now extend your arms straight above your shoulders. Your torso and your arms should make a 90-degree angle and the elbows should be in.  This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, curl the bar down in a semicircular motion until it touches your chin. Squeeze the biceps for a second at the top contracted position. Tip: As you execute this motion only the forearms should move.  At no time should the upper arms be moving at all.  They are to remain perpendicular throughout the movement.
Return to starting position slowly.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: You can also curl down to above your head, rather than to your chin or use an E-Z Bar Attachment instead of a straight one.
Grab a straight bar or E-Z bar attachment that is attached to the low pulley with both hands, using an underhand (palms facing up) shoulder-width grip.
Lie flat on your back on top of an exercise mat in front of the weight stack with your feet flat against the frame of the pulley machine and your legs straight.
With your arms extended and your elbows close to your body slightly bend your arms.  This will be your starting position.
While keeping your upper arms stationary and the elbows close to your body, curl the bar up slowly toward your chest as you breathe out and you squeeze the biceps.
After a second squeeze at the top of the movement, slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: You can also perform this exercise with exercise bands.

Attach a rope to a low cable pulley and adopt a standing, shoulder width stance a couple of feet away from the pulley. Face the machine and take hold of the rope with an overhand grip (little fingers on the rubber ends of the attachment). From the cable pulley to your shoulders, should be a straight line in what will be your starting position.

Exhale and pull the rope in towards your chin. Seperate the rope as much as possible by pulling your little fingers apart and flaring your elbows out, high and wide. Tip: Keep your hands above your elbows and maintain a straight back throughout this movement. Do not rock your bodyweight forward and backwards in order to generate momentum.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the rope along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Cable Rear Delt Rows Rope or Rear Delt Flyes (Machine).

Place a mat in front of a single low cable pulley (around 18 inches away) with the rope attachment connected. Lay down on your back with your head the same end as the cable and have your torso flat with your knees bent at a confortable angle, this will support your lower back.

Grab the rope with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and extend above your chest so your arms are vertical from the floor. Tip: From now on in your elbows should remain as close as possible and your upper arm should be stationary throughout. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, inhale and bend at your elbows creating an arcing motion with the rope so that your hands are heading towards your forehead. The weight should be stretching your triceps. Remember to keep your elbows as close together as possible.
After a brief pause, exhale and return the rope and your hands along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Tricep Rope Extensions or Skull Crushers.

Attach handles to two opposing low cable pulleys. Take a standing, shoulder width stance between the cables facing forward. Take hold of both handles with an underhand grip (palms facing forward) and take a small step forward (around 18 inches). Allow your arms to hang by your sides, torso straight with your chest out. This will be your starting position.

To initiate this movement, exhale and lift the handles on an arcing motion in front of your body until the handles meet in the middle and cross over at shoulder height. Make sure that your elbows are slightly bent throughout and that your arms remain as passive as possible, the movement should be directed through your pectoral muscles (chest).
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handles along the same path back in to the starting positions.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Pec Deck or Flat Dumbbell Flyes.

name_en: Kneeling Single Arm High Cable Rows
instructions_en: Attach a handle to a high cable pulley. Adopt a kneeling position (about 18 inches away from the machine) and take hold of the handle with an overhand grip (palm facing the floor). Once you have hold of the handle, your arm should be at full extension aiming at the high cable pulley (creating a straight line from your shoulder to the pulley). Have a straight back and a 90 degree angle at your knees. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and pull the handle down towards the side of your torso. Use your back muscles in order to activate the movement and keep your arm as passive as possible. Continue to pull the handle until your elbow is either beside or just past your torso (without twisting your body). Tip: Twist your hand as you pull the handle down and in order to get the best contraction.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handle along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap arms.
Variations: Single Arm Row or Single Arm Lat Pulldown.

Adopt a kneeling position underneath a high cable with a rope attached. When setting the weight for this exercise, be cautious as if it is too heavy, your abs will not be taking the load and your lower back will play a part.
Take hold of the ropes and bring them down so they are beside your head on your upper chest. Push your hips back and lean forward to create a 90 degree angle from your lower back and thighs.

From here you should try to arch your back by taking a deep breath, pulling your shoulders back and pushing your chest out. This will be your starting position. Tip: Throughout this movement your hips and your lower limbs should remain stationary.

Exhale and flex your abs and obliques to move your shoulders (and rope) downward towards your right thigh. As you flex your abs and obliques, the arch in your back reverses so that your back is now rounding over. Your elbows should remain as close to your torso as possible throughout.
After a brief pause, inhale and return along the same path to your starting position.
Repeat the movement but this time as you crunch downward, aim towards your left thigh. Keep alternating in this fashion until you have completed the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: If you suffer from lower back pain, be cautious of the resistance.
Variations: Oblique Crunches or Cross Body Crunches.
Attach a rope to a high cable pulley and adopt a kneeling stance facing the cable machine about a 2 feet away.

Take hold of the rope with an overhand grip and keep your arms pointing towards the cable pulley itself. Lean back slightly from your knees allowing you to keep a straight back and looking in the same direction your arms are pointing. This will be your starting position.

Exhale and pull the rope in towards your face. Separate the rope as much as possible by pulling your little fingers apart and flaring your elbows out, high and wide. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout this movement and do not rock your bodyweight forward and backwards in order to generate momentum.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the rope along the same path to the starting position.
Variations: Lat Pulldown or T-Bar Rows With Handle.
Attach a bar to a high cable pulley and adopt a kneeling stance facing away from the machine about a foot away.

Grasping the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing forward), lean forward with your bodyweight so that you create a slight bend at your hips. Your knees should be at 90 degrees located under the bar. Your elbows should also be at 90 degrees, tucked in close to your ears where they should stay throughout the movement. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout, only your forearms should move. This will be your starting position.
To initiate the movement, exhale and press the bar forwards on an arcing path whilst squeezing your triceps. Continue with this movement until your arms are fully extended.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the bar along the same path back into its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bench Dips or Tricep Extension (Rope).

name_en: Kneeling Cable Crunch With Alternating Oblique Twists
instructions_en: Adopt a kneeling position underneath a high cable with a rope attached. When setting the weight for this exercise, be cautious as if it is too heavy, your abs will not be taking the load and your lower back will play a part.
Take hold of the ropes and bring them down so they are beside your head on your upper chest. Push your hips back and lean forward to create a 90 degree angle from your lower back and thighs.

From here you should try to arch your back by taking a deep breath, pulling your shoulders back and pushing your chest out. This will be your starting position. Tip: Throughout this movement your hips and your lower limbs should remain stationary.

Exhale and flex your abs and obliques to move your shoulders (and rope) downward towards your right thigh. As you flex your abs and obliques, the arch in your back reverses so that your back is now rounding over. Your elbows should remain as close to your torso as possible throughout.
After a brief pause, inhale and return along the same path to your starting position.
Repeat the movement but this time as you crunch downward, aim towards your left thigh. Keep alternating in this fashion until you have completed the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: If you suffer from lower back pain, be cautious of the resistance.
Variations: Oblique Crunches or Cross Body Crunches.

Adjust a cable pulley so it is at the top of its runner and set the weight accordingly. Attach a flat bar to the cable. Next take hold of the bar with an underhand grip (palms facing you) and stand away from the cable machine and pull the bar so that there is tension in the cable, elbows close together, above your chest. This will be the starting position of the exercise.To initiate the movement, exhale and pull the bar towards your forehead in an arcing motion. Tip: Only your forearms should move, your upper arms should remain stable. Continue to curl the bar until either your biceps are optimally contracted or you reach your forehead.After a brief pause, inhale and return the bar along the same path back into its starting position.Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.Variations: Cable Curls or Preacher Curls.

Place a handle attachment on to a low cable pulley. Facing away, stand about a foot away from the machine. Adopt a shoulder width, upright standing position, maintaining a straight back throughout. Take hold of the handle in your left hand and return to your original position with your hand hanging perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.

Initiate the movement by exhaling and elevating your left arm on an arcing motion in front of you. Keep a slight bend at your elbow and continue to elevate upward until your arm is parallel with the floor.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your arm along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap arms.
Variation: Front Plate Raise or Alternating Front Raises.

Set two opposing cable pulleys to the bottom of the machine and attach a handle to each. Position a bench in between the two cables and lay on the bench with your chest between the cables. Take hold of the handles and press the weights directly up towards the ceiling until your arms are fully extended (slight bend at your elbows) and the handles are above your chest. This will be your starting position.
Maintaining the slight bend at your elbows, lower your arms in a wide arcing motion until you feel your chest stretching, inhale as you do so.
Following the same path as you went down, squeeze your pectoral muscles (chest) and bring your arms back to the starting position, remembering to exhale as you go.
Pause for a second and then repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Flyes or Pec Deck.

Attach a handle to the cable and adjust the height of the pulley so that it sits at belly button height. Stand side on to the machine and take a step away. Adopt a shoulder width, upright standing position and take hold of the handle with the hand furthest from the machine. Once you have hold of the handle, tuck your elbow in close to your torso (your elbow should be at a 90 degree angle). This will be your starting position.

Exhale and rotate your hand on an imaginary semicricle in front of you, pivoting from your elbow which, remains tight against the side of your torso. Rotate your shoulder fully so that your hand is as close to a right angle (pointing away from your body) as possible.
After a brief pause, return the handle along the same path back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap arms.
Caution: Be cautious with the weight as you could damage your rotator cuff.
Variations: Dumbbell External Rotations or Dynaband External Rotations.
Get a platform of some sort (it can be an aerobics or calf raise platform) that is around 4-6 inches in height.
Place it on the seat of the cable row machine.
Sit down on the machine and place your feet on the front platform or crossbar provided making sure that your knees are slightly bent and not locked.
Lean over as you keep the natural alignment of your back and grab the V-bar handles.

With your arms extended pull back until your torso is at a 90-degree angle from your legs.  Your back should be slightly arched and your chest should be sticking out. You should be feeling a nice stretch on your lats as you hold the bar in front of you.  This is the starting position of the exercise.

Keeping the torso stationary, pull the handles back towards your torso while keeping the arms close to it until you touch the abdominals.  Breathe out as you perform that movement.  At that point you should be squeezing your back muscles hard.  Hold that contraction for a second and slowly go back to the original position while breathing in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Avoid swinging your torso back and forth as you can cause lower back injury by doing so.
Variations: You can use a straight bar instead of a V-Bar and perform with a pronated grip (palms facing down-forward) or a supinated grip (palms facing up-reverse grip).
Start out by placing a flat bench in front of a low pulley cable that has a straight bar attachment.

Use your arms to grab the cable bar with a narrow to shoulder width supinated grip (palms up) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the top of your thighs.  Your wrists should be hanging just beyond your knees.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.  Keep the contraction for a second.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations:

This exercise can also be performed sitting down by kneeling and using the bench as a resting position for your forearms.  Your wrist can hang over the bench and the same movements as mentioned above can be performed.
You can also use a dumbbell instead of a barbell.
Use a twin cable tower. Connect a rope attachment to one cable and an ankle attachment to the other. Both should be positioned at the lowest pulley setting.
Place a mat in between the cables and sit down; attach the ankle strap to your feet.
Lie back and grab the rope with both hands; position the rope behind your head with your hands by your ears.
Make sure you are positioned in the middle with your legs completely outstretched.
In one motion, pull the rope forward in a crunching movement and bring your knees inward to your torso.
As your elbows and knees come together, elevate your shoulders and hips off the floor.

Pause for a moment and in a slow and controlled manner reset to the starting position. You should hold your legs off the ground after the beginning repetition to keep tension on the cable in the resting position.
Repeat the same movement to failure.

Tip: For maximum challenge and effectiveness, lower your legs as close to the floor as possible without touching before going into the crunch. In the lowered position, your legs should also be completely straight. Don’t cheat yourself by bending your knees.

Adjust two opposing cable pulleys so they are at the bottom of their runners and set the weights accordingly. Position yourself in between these runners with a shoulder width stance. Attach a handle to each cable and (whilst maintaining good posture (a straight back)), bend at your knees and hips to lower your torso, enabling you to take hold of each. Press through your heels and quads whilst simultaneously using the muscles in your back to bring your torso into a vertical position. This will be the starting position of the exercise.
To initiate the movement, exhale and elevate your shoulders by pulling them in towards your ears. Continue to elevate until the muscles around your neck (trapezius) are optimally contracted.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handles along the same path back into their starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Shrugs or Smith Machine Shrugs.

Attach handles to two low cables. Take one in each hand and face away from the machine. Bring the handles up to shoulder height with your elbows are flared out wide and at a 90 degree angle. Adopt a standing, shoulder width stance (you can split your stance for more stability), maintaining a straight back throughout the movement. Your palms should be facing forward. This will be your starting position.
Whilst exhaling, press both handles towards the ceiling until your elbow becomes fully extended (straight).
After a brief pause, return the handles back along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Shoulder Press or Seated Shoulder Press.

Adjust two opposing cable pulleys so they are at the bottom of their runners and set the weights accordingly. Next, attach handles to each cable and place a flat bench length ways between the cables. Take a seat on the bench facing away from the machines with your knees bent at a 90 degree angle and your torso leaning forwards whilst maintaining a straight back. Next you will need to cross the cables over underneath the bench so your left hand takes hold of the right cable and vice versa. Adopt underhand grips and allow your elbows to flare out wide. This is your starting position.
To initiate the movement, exhale and pull the back of your hands out and away from one another on a wide arcing path by flexing the muscles in the rear of your shoulders or across your upper back.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handles back along the same path to their starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Lateral Raises or Seated Side Lateral Raises.

Adjust a cable pulley so that it is at the top of its runner and set the weight accordingly. Next, attach a rope to the cable and place a flat bench adjacent to the runner (around a foot away). Take a seat on the bench facing away from the machine with your knees and hips at 90 degree angles and your torso upright. Reach up and grab the rope with both hands, pulling it down to sit either side of your head on your upper chest. This is your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and flex your abs to move your shoulders (and rope) downward towards your thighs. As you flex your abs, the arch in your back reverses so that your back is now rounding over. Your elbows should remain as close to your torso as possible throughout.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the rope back along the same path to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Cable Crunch or Cocoons.

Adjust a cable pulley so that it is 2/3 of the way up its runner and set the weight accordingly. Take a large step away from the cable and set your stance at shoulder width. Take hold of the cable in both hands and extend your arms in front of you. This is your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and rotate through your core to turn your shoulders away from the cable machine. Keep your arms extended as you rotate and continue until your arms are pointing away from the cable machine.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the handle along the same path back into its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Russian Twists or Hip Crossovers.

Attach two handles to a low cable pulley, take hold of them with a neutral grip and sit about 2 feet away from the pulley system. Once seated, you should have your arms and legs out in front of you (slightly bent) with your posture held in an upright position (this is paramount to maintain throughout the movement) with your chest pushed out. This is your starting position.

Exhale and pull the handles in towards your lower abdominals by contracting your back muscles. Your posture should remain upright throughout the movement and your arms should remain as passive as possible.
Once the handles touch your abdominals, pause for a brief moment before inhaling and returning them along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: If you suffer from back pain, this may not be a suitable movement for you.
Variations: Landmine T Bar Rows or Underhand Cable Pulldowns.

Attach a rope to a high cable pulley and adopt a standing, shoulder width stance a couple of feet away from the pulley. Face the machine and take hold of the rope with an overhand grip (little fingers on the rubber ends of the attachment).
Elevate your arms so that they are just above parallel with the floor. This will be your starting position.

Exhale and pull the rope in towards your upper chest. Seperate the rope as much as possible by pulling your little fingers apart and flaring your elbows out high and wide. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout this movement and do not rock your bodyweight forward and backwards in order to generate momentum.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the rope along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Facepulls or Rear Delt Flyes (Machine)

Attach a rope to a low cable pulley, take hold of the rope and stand with an upright, shoulder width stance facing away from the machine, arms straight above your head with your little fingers on the rubber ends to the rope. Keep your elbows tucked in close to your ears and have your knuckles facing the ceiling. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout this movement and keep your elbows pinned in close to your ears. This will be your starting position.
Whilst your upper arms remain stationary, inhale and lower the rope down on an arcing motion behind your head until your elbows are at an acute angle (less than 90 degrees).
Pause for a brief moment then exhale and return the rope along the same path to the starting position whilst contracting your triceps.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Skullcrushers or Bench Dips.
Attach two handles to a low cable pulley and place a mat in front of it. Lay down on your back and securely put the handles over your toes.

Lift your feet off the ground so that your knees and hips are both at 90 degrees and place your hands on your temples. Tip: Try not to pull your neck or head in to the movement with your hands. If your neck starts to ache, put one hand behind your head for support and to take some weight.
Now exhale and whilst crunching your up torso upward, bring your knees in towards your chest.
Once your abs are as tight as they can be pause for a brief moment, inhale and return your body back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Cocoons or Jack-Knives.
Remove the handles from two parallel cables. Adjust the height of the pulleys so they are above your head and adopt a standing, shoulder width stance between them.

Once you have done this take hold of the left cable with your right hand and the right cable with your left hand. Your arms should be at full extension with a slight bend at your elbows. This will be your starting position.
